115 - This Is How You Avoid Making Those Newbie Mistakes - Sales Tips - YouTube.mp4
Are you a brand new salesperson and this is your first year, or maybe looking at getting your first sales job and you don't want to make the mistakes that most new salespeople make? Today, we're going to go over the untold truth of your first year in selling. Now, my name is Jeremy Miner. I'm the chairman and founder of 7th Level. Now, I'm going to give you a few things that you want to look out for. I'm going to give you the top three mistakes that brand new salespeople make. Had I known these my first year, oh, I would have made way more freaking money than I did, right? First of all, go down to the bottom of this video and hit the subscribe button. That's probably important for you.
Hit the subscribe button and to the right of that or maybe the left, I don't know, somewhere in there, there's like a little bell. That's your notifications button. Hit the notifications button as well so you get notified by YouTube every time I post a new training video, which I typically do two to four times a week. The first thing is a lot of salespeople do this. They're like, you know what, I'm going to learn how to sell after I get my job in selling. Like, I'm going to go get the job and then I'm going to learn how to sell once I get the job. Help me understand here. That would be like a doctor, like a neurosurgeon.
That's kind of like a neurosurgeon saying, well, I'm going to learn how to do the brain surgeries on patients like after I get the job as a brain surgeon. I don't think anybody's going to hire that person. And here's the thing, employees are starting to get smart. As the recession that's about to hit the fan, as the economy contracts, they get pickier and pickier on who they hire because they don't want to hire people who don't know how to sell. So, as a salesperson, you want to acquire the necessary skills so on day number one, when you pick up the phone and start dialing, or you have that first appointment on Zoom, or you go to that first prospect's home, or you walk into that place of business where the business owner is, you probably want to know what to do, what to say, and ask where you actually succeed, where you actually start to make sales
Because here's the problem, if you start day number one and you don't have these skills and you don't know what the hell you're doing because it's not like you're going to learn those skills in one day on the job, and you don't know what you're doing when you don't make any sales the first couple of days or maybe the first week or the first couple of weeks or the first month, you don't make hardly any sales. Well, first of all, it sucks for you because you're not making that much money, and second of all, what do you think is going on in the business owner's mind or your sales manager's mind or the vice president of sales mind that's over you?
Well, you're going to get let go pretty quick, and it's going to be really, really, really freaking hard for you to get another job because you didn't take advantage of learning how to sell before you got your first sales job. So you want to go out, you want to acquire the skills, you want to acquire the skills from the right people, you want to acquire skills that work with human behavior rather than against it so that way, day number one, you actually start making sales. You start making money, you look good towards your boss, people are going to be like, wow, the new guy, the new girl, she's pretty damn good. You don't want to be the new person like, oh yeah, that new person, they kind of suck
or they start to get rid of you So take my advice on this: learn how to sell before you get the sales job because a lot of employers will ask you like how much money you've personally spent on learning advanced skills so you could sell more. If you're like, well, I bought a book last year, 'How to Win Friends and Influence People' for $10 and I saw a couple of videos on YouTube, are they going to hire you over the person that invested maybe a few thousand bucks in a course or went to some seminars, bought 10 or 12 or 14 books for that year? I think you know the answer. Okay, now, number two: second thing you want to do, you want to hang out.
You don't want to hang out with this. The biggest mistake I see a lot of brand new salespeople make, and I made this mistake when I first started, is I was hanging out with the wrong type of people, right? I was like the new guy who wasn't very good, so I was drifting towards all the other not so good new salespeople. It's really hard to learn when you're learning from the wrong people. All right? So you don't want to hang out with average salespeople, and even be careful about your management, who you're hanging out because a lot of times sales managers might not necessarily be great at selling, but they're just really good at managing people.
I see a lot of brand new salespeople think, oh my gosh, my sales manager must be the best salesperson ever because they're the manager, when the opposite is true. Then maybe they're average or above average at selling, but they're just really good at managing people. So you have to be very selective on who you associate yourself with, all right? So bad— here's what I always see— bad advice kills reps' careers in sales more than anything, all right? Because they don't know what they're doing, so they start taking advice from salespeople who aren't that great or sales managers who weren't that good at selling, and they just think that whatever they're told is true somehow. It's like going to a doctor, and the doctor says, hey, you know, you should do this, this, and this without getting other opinions from other doctors who might be better, who might be more qualified.
It's not like every doctor has the same breadth of skill level, and the same is true in selling. So you gotta be really careful on who you're taking your advice from, okay? You wanna take advice from salespeople who are making a freaking ton of money. Now, you also don't wanna be annoying. Let me give you some things, because when I got into sales, I became very successful very quickly because of my background in behavioral science and human psychology. So I started making, you know, by my second year, I was already making like five, six hundred thousand a year in commissions by like my second year So I would always have new salespeople come up to me, and they would badger me and pester me, and I'm like, oh my gosh, you know, like I don't have time to talk to you.
However, there were a few salespeople that were smart, and what they did is they would do favors for me and not ask for anything in return, right? I would walk in, and they'd be like, hey, dude, I just thought I'd stop and say hi. I don't have much going on my calendar. Is there anything I can help you out with today? And they would never ask me for sales advice in the beginning, anything I help you out with today, or hey, you know, I'm going across the street to Starbucks. Do you want something? I can pick something up for you. Sure, and they'd buy me like a, you know, a sandwich or something like that. And when they did that without asking for anything in return, guess what I did?
Hey, do you want some help? Like, I know you're brand new here. Like, do you have questions for me? And magically, those new reps started doing what? Making way more sales than all the other reps who were in the break room, hanging out with all the average salespeople who were complaining about their leads and prospects. So you want to take advice from people who actually were very, very successful. And I even see that in salespeople with sales trainers. Like, how do you know the sales trainers you're learning from were really, really successful at sales? Do you know that? Like, have you seen their W-2s? Did you see their 1099s when they were a sales rep in the trenches like you? Or do you just see them like, oh, well, they must be successful because their company's really successful?
Well, maybe they're just a really good marketer. Maybe they weren't that great at selling. So, and I did the same thing. When I first got into sales, I started taking advice from sales trainers, and then after a while, I started finding out the sales trainers' backgrounds I'm like, oh, that guy wasn't even in the top 10 or 15 people in his company. And they only had like 50 reps. He was above average, but he wasn't great. Like, he only made like 150,000 a year. Now he's a world-class sales trainer. It's not like you're going to learn 100% of what that person knows. Maybe you only learn 50%. So you're going to make half of what they do. If you want to make more than that, you have to learn from somebody who's actually made that.
Like, if you're learning from a sales trainer who only made 150 grand a year as a salesperson, how are you going to make 300,000 a year You can't because they don't have the skills to teach you how to do that. So you want to learn from somebody or other people who've made hundreds of thousands of dollars a year, if not millions a year in commissions as a sales rep working for a company just like you are. That's who you want to learn from because that's going to get you the highest level. I can assure you of that, okay? And then the last thing is I see so many brand new salespeople easily distracted. They just get distracted, okay?
They get into the office, they get on social media, they look at their friends' Cats, and they look at the cool girl they went out with the other day, or vice versa, the girl looks at the guy, and they just start daydreaming. And then all of a sudden, 15 minutes later, then they get on their emails and start looking at their emails and responding for 15 or 20 minutes. And then somebody comes, knocks on the door, and like, hey, do you want to go to lunch? Yeah, sure, and they start chit-chatting for five or 10 minutes. And then all of a sudden, 45 minutes into the day, they hadn't even made one sales call. It's like, what are you doing?
And then at lunch, instead of taking, well, I would only take about 15 or 20 minutes when I was at lunch because I wanted to make money. They take like 30 minutes, or 45, or an hour and 15, or an hour and a half. And then they get back in and they ate a lot of food, so they're really tired. They get back on social media for another 15 or 20 minutes, read some emails for another 15 or 20 minutes, and then before you know it, out of that eight-hour workday, they might have worked four hours, if that. That's not gonna get you a lot of success in sales. I hate to tell you that. So you want to set out time.
We call that prospect time blocking Okay, now before I do that, if I say, when I say don't get on social media, now, if you are comfortable on video, which you should be, you can practice to be, and you start doing reels, talking about different things in your industry, and things to look out for, and things to avoid, and good things like that. People start to view you as an expert with what you offer. So let's say if you're like a life insurance agent and you start talking about the different types of policies and typical, like, you know, I'm gonna give you the three biggest mistakes that the average person makes when they purchase a home and they don't have a financial protection to protect that equity in the home.
Today I'm gonna go over blah, blah, blah, blah, blah. And people start seeing those reels day in and day out, day in and day out, day in and day out. Who do you think they're gonna go to when they're looking for an insurance policy? You, or a car, you, or a home. If you're real estate, you. So doing reels, even if you sold like medical device equipment or cybersecurity, doesn't matter if you sell B2B or B2C, doing reels like that, doing one or two every single day, seven days a week, it takes work, but you can generate a lot of easy lay down sales from that. So you wanna, well, like I also went back here, you wanna practice what's called prospect time blocking.
So you wanna set certain hours of your day where there's no distractions, nobody can bother you, and all you're doing is you're focused on what I call income-producing activities. That's the last thing. Average salespeople are not focused on income-producing activities. They're more busy entering in data all day in their computer, right? Talking like a lot of notes about each call. That doesn't really matter. They're talking with the other average salespeople out in the break room. They're on social media. They're just not doing income-producing activities. Income-producing activities, in your mind, what are they They're prospecting, they're booking appointments, they're handling your inbound leads, they're handling your outbound leads, they're doing second calls, third calls, depending on what you sell, right? They're doing follow-ups. I don't like the word follow-up, but you know what I mean by that.
They're getting with their leads. If you're focused only on income-producing activities all day, by default, you're going to outsell people in your industry, in your office, that are only working half the time because they're so easily distracted. So there you go. There are three things you want to avoid in the first year of selling, or any, really, any year. I even see vets doing that sometimes, and that's why they don't make a lot of money.
